1: 'He led me to the gate facing east' — the east gate that has been central throughout the vision (40:6 as the entry point; Ezekiel 44:1-2 as permanently sealed after the glory's return). The east gate of the temple faced the sunrise and, in Ezekiel's vision, faced the direction from which the glory of YHWH would return. In ancient Near Eastern religious geography, the east was the direction of the divine sunrise, new beginnings, and divine theophany. Temples were frequently oriented east-west so that the rising sun would illuminate the inner sanctuary.
2: 'The glory of the God of Israel was coming from the east. His voice was like the sound of many waters, and the land was bright with his glory' — the theophanic return, reversing the departure of Ezekiel 10-11. The glory had departed eastward (Ezekiel 10:18-19; Ezekiel 11:22-23); it returns from the east. The voice like many waters (H4325 + H7227, kol mayim rabbim) echoes the initial vision of Ezekiel 1:24 ('a voice like the sound of many waters') and anticipates the voice of the risen Christ in Revelation 1:15. The brightness illuminating the entire land (haaretz) contrasts with the darkness that fell on Jerusalem at the glory's departure.
3: 'The vision I saw was like the vision I had seen when he came to destroy the city, and like the vision I had seen by the Chebar canal' — Ezekiel's self-referential connection to his three great visions: (1) the Chebar chariot vision (Ezekiel 1); (2) the judgment-of-Jerusalem vision (Ezekiel 8-11, also involving the glory's departure); (3) now the restoration vision of Ezekiel 40-48. The three visions form a theological arc: call/commission (Ezekiel 1), judgment/departure (Ezekiel 8-11), restoration/return (Ezekiel 40-48). The same theophanic vehicle — the chariot-throne — appears in all three, providing continuity across the entire book.
4: 'The glory of the LORD entered the temple by the gate facing east' — the precise reversal of the departure. Ezekiel 10:18-19 describes the glory going out by the east gate of the temple; here it enters the temple by the east gate. The same spatial language of exit and entrance marks the theological reversal: the exile is over at the moment the glory returns. The temple filled with divine glory is the eschatological endpoint of the entire prophetic movement that began with the glory's departure.
5: 'The Spirit lifted me up and brought me into the inner court, and behold, the glory of the LORD filled the temple' — the Spirit's transportation of Ezekiel to the inner court to witness the filling of the temple. The glory filling the temple parallels Solomon's temple dedication in 1 Kings 8:10-11: 'when the priests came out of the holy place, a cloud filled the house of the LORD, so that the priests could not stand to minister because of the cloud, for the glory of the LORD filled the house of the LORD.' The same event of glory-filling that inaugurated the first temple now inaugurates the eschatological temple.
6: 'While the man was standing beside me, I heard one speaking to me from within the temple' — the speaking voice from within the filled temple is YHWH himself, whose voice comes from the place of his glory-presence. The 'man' (the angelic measuring guide) remains beside Ezekiel while the divine voice speaks directly. The distinction between the guide (who measured and explained) and the divine voice (who now speaks) marks the transition from architectural vision to theological instruction.
7: 'Son of man, this is the place of my throne and the place of the soles of my feet, where I will dwell in the midst of Israel forever' — the throne/footstool theology: YHWH's throne (kisse) is in the temple, the soles of his feet resting on earth in the temple precincts (cf. Psalms 132:7: 'let us go to his dwelling place; let us worship at his footstool'; 1 Chronicles 28:2: David saying 'I had it in my heart to build a house as a place of rest for the ark of the covenant of the LORD, the footstool of our God'). The throne-in-the-temple and footstool-of-God imagery is consistent with the ancient Near Eastern understanding of the temple as the palace of the deity, where the divine king rules.
8: 'By setting their threshold by my threshold and their doorpost beside my doorpost, with only a wall between me and them' — the architectural complaint about the previous temple arrangements: the royal palace was built immediately adjacent to the temple compound, with shared walls and thresholds. The kings of Judah had effectively integrated the palace into the sacred precinct, blurring the boundary between royal and divine space. Archaeological evidence from Megiddo and Jerusalem suggests that Iron Age Israelite royal complexes were built in direct adjacency to cult sites.
9: 'Now let them put away their prostitution and the dead bodies of their kings far from me, and I will dwell in their midst forever' — two specific defilements: (1) the prostitution/idolatry (zenutam) of the monarchy's syncretistic religion; (2) the dead bodies of the kings placed in proximity to the temple (David was buried 'in the city of David' adjacent to the temple mount, 1 Kings 2:10; subsequent Davidic kings similarly buried nearby, 1 Kings 11:43; Ezekiel 14:31). Burial of the dead near the sacred precinct created ritual defilement; the kings' tombs as a source of corpse-impurity contaminating the divine dwelling.
10: 'As for you, son of man, describe the temple to the house of Israel, that they may be ashamed of their iniquities' — the theological purpose of the temple vision stated explicitly: the vision of the ideal temple is intended to produce shame in Israel. The contrast between the desecrated historical temple (as revealed in Ezekiel 8-11) and the perfect eschatological temple (revealed in Ezekiel 40-48) is meant to generate kelimah (shame/humiliation) and cherpah (reproach/disgrace) — the interior movement of repentance. The architecture itself is a theological argument.
11: 'And if they are ashamed of all they have done, then make known to them the plan of the temple — its arrangement and all its exits and entrances, all its forms and all its statutes' — the conditional disclosure: if they are ashamed, then give them the full technical specification. The shame is the precondition for receiving the restoration promise. This reverses the normal order of proclamation and response: the vision is first presented to generate the proper interior response; only then is the complete technical specification shared. The architecture is both inducement to shame and gift to the repentant.
12: 'This is the law of the temple: the whole territory on the top of the mountain all around shall be most holy. This is the law of the temple' — the summary principle of the entire temple complex: the mountain-top territory is wholly most holy (qodesh qadashim). The principle is stated in a bracket (beginning and end of Ezekiel 43:12) for emphasis. Unlike the previous temple on Zion (which had graduated zones from most holy to holy to common), the entire eschatological temple mountain is qodesh qadashim. The eschatological intensification of holiness: what was restricted to the inner sanctuary now characterizes the entire precinct.
13: 'These are the measurements of the altar by long cubits' — the altar dimensions using the long cubit (7 handbreadths). The altar base: one cubit high, one cubit wide, with a rim of one span around the edge (gev, H1354, the rim/border of the altar). The base (cheyq ha'aretz, literally 'the bosom of the earth') grounds the altar in the earth, creating the foundation from which the successive levels rise. The altar structure rises from the earth to the divine — the architectural expression of the sacrificial theology of elevation toward YHWH.
14: 'From the base on the ground to the lower ledge, two cubits, with a breadth of one cubit; and from the lower ledge to the upper ledge, four cubits, with a breadth of one cubit' — the two-level ledge structure of the altar. Each ledge-to-ledge transition represents a step in the elevation toward the altar hearth. The ledge widths of one cubit each provide the stepped platform on which officiating priests could stand. Stepped altar construction is attested archaeologically at Megiddo (the large round altar) and Beer-sheba (the horned stone altar) from the Iron Age period.
15: 'And the altar hearth, four cubits high; and from the altar hearth projecting upward, four horns' — the altar hearth (ari'el, H739, literally 'lion of God' or 'hearth of God' — from Akkadian arallu, the underworld, or Aramaic ariel, a sacrificial term). The word appears in Isaiah 29:1-2 where Jerusalem itself is called Ariel, and in the Mesha Stele (line 12) where it refers to a cult object from Nebo. The four horns (qarnaot) projecting upward from the corners of the altar hearth: the horns were the most important cultic feature of ancient Israelite altars (cf. Exodus 27:2; 1 Kings 2:28 — grasping the horn of the altar for sanctuary).
16: 'The altar hearth shall be twelve cubits long and twelve cubits broad, square on all four sides' — the twelve-by-twelve altar hearth continuing the square-plan principle throughout the entire complex (Holy of Holies 20×20, inner court 100×100, outer precinct 500×500, and now altar hearth 12×12). The square altar echoes the requirement of Exodus 27:1 for the tabernacle altar (5×5 cubits, square). The twelve-cubit dimension corresponds to the twelve tribes of Israel — the altar as the gathering point of the entire covenant people before YHWH.
17: 'The upper ledge shall be fourteen cubits long and fourteen cubits broad on all four sides, with a rim of half a cubit and a base of one cubit all around' — the upper ledge extending two cubits beyond the altar hearth on each side (12+2=14) and a drainage gutter (cheyq) at the bottom. The drainage channel was essential for the blood of sacrifices: the altar was the site of massive amounts of sacrificial blood, and a drainage system was required. The bronze altar in Solomon's temple stood on a ten-cubit-square bronze base (1 Kings 8:64). The altar faces east — the divine direction throughout the entire vision.
18: 'Son of man, thus says the Lord GOD: These are the ordinances for the altar on the day when it is made, for offering burnt offerings on it and sprinkling blood on it' — the transition from architectural specification to ritual ordinances. The altar is built not as an aesthetic object but as a functional instrument of sacrifice: burnt offerings and blood-sprinkling inaugurate its service. The transition from measurement to ordinance parallels the Sinai pattern: after the tabernacle's construction specifications (Exod 25-31), the ordination and dedication rituals follow (Exod 29; Lev 8-9).
19: 'You shall give to the Levitical priests of the family of Zadok, who draw near to me to minister to me, declares the Lord GOD, a bull from the herd for a sin offering' — the Zadokite priests receive the primary altar-service role in the eschatological temple. Zadok was the priest who remained loyal to David during Absalom's rebellion (2 Samuel 15:24-29) and to Solomon over against Abiathar during Adonijah's attempted coup (1 Kings 1:7-8; Ezekiel 2:26-27). His family received the high-priestly succession; the Zadokites are the legitimate priestly line in Ezekiel's theology (cf. Ezekiel 44:15-16 for the extended theological treatment of the Zadokite priesthood).
20: 'You shall take some of its blood and put it on the four horns of the altar, on the four corners of the upper ledge, and on the rim all around; thus you shall purify it and make atonement for it' — the blood ritual for altar consecration. Blood applied to the four horns, the four corners, and the rim: comprehensive coverage of the altar's most prominent features. The application of blood to horns (the most sacred element of the altar) and to the structural extremities defines the altar as a consecrated instrument. The consecration procedure (chitta, H2398, purification offering → purify) and atonement (kippur, H3722, cover/atone) transform the constructed altar from an inert object to an active instrument of the covenant relationship.
21: 'You shall take the bull of the sin offering and burn it in the designated place of the temple, outside the sanctuary' — the disposal of the purification-offering animal outside the sanctuary proper. The burning of the sin offering's body outside the sanctuary is the Levitical rule for offerings where blood was brought into the sanctuary (Leviticus 4:12; Ezekiel 6:30; Ezekiel 16:27). The 'designated place' (miphqad habbayit, the appointed/place of the house) is a zone for consuming the purification sacrifice without contaminating the altar area.
22: 'On the second day you shall offer a male goat without blemish for a sin offering, and the altar shall be purified as it was purified with the bull' — the seven-day consecration sequence begins: day one (bull), day two (male goat). The repetition of the purification with different animals on successive days is consistent with Levitical practice for major consecrations (Lev 8-9: Aaron's ordination used seven days of offering). The change of animal (bull → male goat) within the same purification framework provides variety in the sacrificial presentation while maintaining the consistent atonement theology.
23: 'When you have finished purifying it, you shall offer a bull from the herd without blemish and a ram from the flock without blemish' — after the preliminary purification (days 1-2), the ongoing consecration continues with burnt offerings (bull and ram). The shift from sin offering to burnt offering marks the transition from removing impurity to presenting positive worship. The 'without blemish' (tamim, H8549, whole/complete/blameless) requirement applies to all sacrificial animals (Leviticus 1:3,10; Ezekiel 3:1; Ezekiel 22:18-22) and reflects the principle that only what is complete and whole is appropriate for presentation to YHWH.
24: 'You shall present them before the LORD, and the priests shall throw salt on them and offer them as a burnt offering to the LORD' — the salt requirement for all altar offerings (Leviticus 2:13: 'you shall season all your grain offerings with salt; you shall not omit from your grain offering the salt of the covenant with your God'). Salt represents the preservative quality of the covenant — incorruptible, eternal, purifying. The 'covenant of salt' language (Numbers 18:19; 2 Chronicles 13:5) makes salt an explicit symbol of covenant fidelity. Every burnt offering seasoned with salt is a statement of covenant renewal.
25: 'For seven days you shall provide daily a goat for a sin offering; also a bull from the herd and a ram from the flock without blemish' — the full seven-day consecration period. The seven-day structure is the standard ancient Near Eastern festival period for temple dedication: Solomon's temple dedication included seven days (1 Kings 8:65); the Mesopotamian New Year festival (Akitu) was a multi-day ceremony that included temple purification. The seven days of altar consecration transform the newly constructed altar into the living instrument of YHWH's covenant service.
26: 'Seven days shall they make atonement for the altar, cleanse it, and so consecrate it' — the triple action of the seven-day process: kipper (atone/cover), tiharu (cleanse/purify), millu yedav (fill its hands — the consecration idiom, literally 'fill the hands' of the altar, the same phrase used for priestly ordination in Exodus 28:41; Ezekiel 29:9,29,35). The altar is 'ordained' like a priest — filled with hands (given its portion) and thereby authorized for service. The altar as an ordained instrument shares the language of ordination with the ordained priesthood.
27: 'And when these days are completed, from the eighth day onward the priests shall offer your burnt offerings and your peace offerings on the altar, and I will accept you, declares the Lord GOD' — the eighth day as the first day of regular altar service, after the seven-day consecration. The seventh-day + eighth-day structure is a theological pattern: the sabbath (seventh day) of consecration gives way to the new beginning (eighth day) of ongoing service. Aaron's ordination (Leviticus 9:1) similarly began on the 'eighth day.' The declaration 'I will accept you' (veratziythi etchem) is the climactic statement of the entire altar-consecration: YHWH's acceptance of the worship offered at the newly consecrated altar. This acceptance is what the entire restoration project aims at — the restored people worshiping at the restored altar, accepted by the restored divine presence.
